---
title: "Lab 11: Capstone — Event-Driven Procedural Generation in the Cloud"
page-layout: full
include-in-header:
  text: |
    <style>
      /* Stop Quarto's mermaid-init.js from forcing width=100% on the SVG,
         which scales the whole diagram (and its text) down to container width.
         Forcing auto sizing lets the SVG render at its intrinsic px size, so
         per-subtype font sizes (actorFontSize, messageFontSize, etc.) are
         actually visible. Horizontal scroll on narrow viewports is acceptable. */
      svg.mermaid-js,
      figure.figure svg.mermaid-js {
        width: auto !important;
        height: auto !important;
        max-width: none !important;
      }
    </style>
---


::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## readme.txt — Lab Objectives

- For our final lab, you will deploy an event-driven cloud architecture.
- Instead of building a simple, monolithic web server that does everything (and crashes when things get heavy), you will build a **decoupled system**.
- We are going to deploy an application that uses the [**Wave Function Collapse (WFC)**](https://github.com/mxgmn/WaveFunctionCollapse) algorithm.
- WFC is a computationally heavy mathematical algorithm used in game design to procedurally generate maps and textures.
- Because this algorithm requires massive amounts of RAM and computation time, it should not be run on a typical web server.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Architecture & Data Flow

You will wire together several distinct Google Cloud services:

1. **App Engine (The Frontend):** A lightweight web portal where users upload a "seed" image and request a generated world.
2. **Cloud Storage (The Filing Cabinet):** Two buckets—one to hold the user's input image, and one to hold the final generated map.
3. **Firestore (The Whiteboard):** A serverless NoSQL database that tracks the status of the generation jobs (e.g., "PENDING", "COMPLETE").
4. **Pub/Sub (The Queue):** A message broker. The frontend drops a "Work Order" into this queue and immediately returns a loading screen to the user.
5. **Cloud Run (The Heavy Worker):** An auto-scaling container packed with 8GB of RAM. It listens to the queue, pulls down the work order, runs the heavy Numba/Python math, saves the result, and updates Firestore.
6. **GitHub Actions (CI/CD):** An automated pipeline that deploys code updates using Zero-Trust security (Workload Identity Federation).

The full data flow is illustrated in this sequence diagram:

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {
  "theme": "default",
  "themeVariables": {
    "fontFamily": "Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif"
  },
  "sequence": {
    "useMaxWidth": false,
    "actorFontSize": 30,
    "actorFontWeight": "bold",
    "noteFontSize": 26,
    "messageFontSize": 26,
    "messageFontWeight": "500",
    "width": 150,
    "boxMargin": 24,
    "boxTextMargin": 10,
    "noteMargin": 22,
    "messageMargin": 65,
    "mirrorActors": true,
    "wrap": true
  }
}}%%
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User / Browser
    participant FE as Frontend (App Engine)
    participant IB as Input Bucket (GCS)
    participant FS as Firestore (wfc-db)
    participant PS as Pub/Sub (Work Queue)
    participant BW as Backend Worker (Cloud Run)
    participant OB as Output Bucket (GCS)

    Note over U, FE: Phase 1: Job Submission (Sync)
    U->>FE: Submit Seed Image & Options
    activate FE
    FE->>IB: Upload seed.png (job_id)
    FE->>FS: Create job_id, status: PENDING
    FE->>PS: Publish Work Order (JSON)
    FE-->>U: Job ID Queued
    deactivate FE

    Note over U, FE: Phase 2: Job Polling (Async)
    loop Every 3 Seconds
        U->>FE: Poll /status/job_id
        activate FE
        FE->>FS: Read job_id status
        FS-->>FE: Status (PENDING, etc.)
        FE-->>U: Current Job State
        deactivate FE
    end

    Note over PS, BW: Phase 3: Job Processing (Event-Driven)
    PS-)BW: Push Message (HTTP POST)
    activate BW
    Note right of BW: heavy WFC math (Numba-compiled)
    alt Success
        BW->>IB: Download seed.png
        BW->>OB: Upload generated.png
        BW->>FS: Update status: COMPLETE, output_url
    else Error / Timeout
        BW->>FS: Update status: ERROR / TIMED OUT
    end
    BW-->>PS: HTTP 200 OK (Ack)
    deactivate BW
```

You have been provided the application code (Python, HTML, JavaScript) in the starter repository. Your job is to act as the Cloud Architect: you must provision the infrastructure using Terraform, establish the security bindings, and deploy the services in the correct sequence.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 1: Ground Zero — Setup

::: {.callout-important collapse="false"}
## A Fresh Project Is Required

Because of the complexity of this architecture and the strict IAM (Identity and Access Management) rules required for CI/CD, **you must create a brand new Google Cloud Project for this lab.**
:::

### 1.1 Create a New GCP Project
1. Go to the Google Cloud Console.
2. Create a new project named `wfc-capstone-[YOUR-INITIALS]`.
3. Ensure billing is linked to this new project.
4. Click the **Activate Cloud Shell** icon (the terminal prompt `>_` in the top right corner of the console). We will use Cloud Shell for this lab instead of your local machine because it has Terraform and the Google Cloud CLI pre-installed.
5. Ensure your Cloud Shell is connected to your new project:
   ```bash
   gcloud config set project [YOUR_NEW_PROJECT_ID]
   ```

### 1.2 Fork and Clone the Starter Repo
Navigate to the starter repository: `https://github.com/JacobDowns/wfc_cloud`

1. **Fork** the repository to your own GitHub account.
2. **Clone** your forked repository directly into your **Cloud Shell** terminal. Using Cloud Shell means the entire codebase is already in your Google Cloud environment—no copying and pasting files from your local machine required!
   ```bash
   git clone [https://github.com/YourGitHubUsername/wfc_cloud.git](https://github.com/YourGitHubUsername/wfc_cloud.git)
   cd wfc_cloud
   ```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 2: Understanding the Infrastructure (Terraform)

Open the `infrastructure/main.tf` file in your cloned repository. Take 5 minutes to read through the resource blocks. Notice how Infrastructure as Code (IaC) defines the relationships between the services:

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Infrastructure Breakdown

* **The Buckets:** It creates `wfc-inputs` and `wfc-outputs`. Crucially, it attaches an `iam_member` policy to make them publicly readable so the web dashboard can display the images.
* **The Pub/Sub Queue:** It creates a topic and a "Push Subscription." This tells Google Cloud to actively push messages to your Cloud Run URL whenever a new job arrives.
* **Zero-Trust Security:** It creates a specific Service Account (`wfc-pubsub-invoker`). The Pub/Sub queue wears this "ID badge" when it talks to Cloud Run, and Cloud Run is configured to *only* accept traffic from this specific badge.
* **Workload Identity Federation (WIF):** At the bottom, Terraform sets up an OIDC Provider. This allows GitHub Actions to deploy to your GCP project using temporary cryptographic tokens instead of permanent, insecure JSON keys.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 3: The Deployment Sequence

In distributed systems, you encounter "Chicken and Egg" problems. For example: Terraform needs the Cloud Run URL to set up the Pub/Sub push subscription, but you can't run Terraform until you have that URL! We will follow a strict deployment sequence to solve this.

### 3.1 Initialize Firestore
Firestore must be initialized manually once per project. Run this in Cloud Shell:
```bash
gcloud firestore databases create --database="wfc-db" --location=us-west1 --type=firestore-native
```

### 3.2 The Initial Backend Deployment (Manual)
We need to deploy the backend worker manually this *first* time just to get its public URL for Terraform. 

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## The "Secure-by-Default" Fix
Google Cloud recently updated its security policies so that default service accounts are created with zero permissions. Because `gcloud run deploy` uses Cloud Build behind the scenes, we need to explicitly grant the build account permission to access Cloud Storage and Artifact Registry before we can deploy our code.
:::

Run these commands sequentially in Cloud Shell:

```bash
# 1. Enable the required APIs for building from source AND Terraform planning
gcloud services enable cloudbuild.googleapis.com run.googleapis.com artifactregistry.googleapis.com cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com

# 2. Grab your Project ID and Project Number
PROJECT_ID=$(gcloud config get-value project)
PROJECT_NUMBER=$(gcloud projects describe $PROJECT_ID --format="value(projectNumber)")

# 3. Grant the build account permission to read source code and save containers
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding $PROJECT_ID \
    --member="serviceAccount:${PROJECT_NUMBER}-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" \
    --role="roles/storage.admin"

gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding $PROJECT_ID \
    --member="serviceAccount:${PROJECT_NUMBER}-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" \
    --role="roles/artifactregistry.admin"

# 4. Deploy the worker!
cd worker/
gcloud run deploy wfc-worker \
    --source . \
    --region us-west1 \
    --no-allow-unauthenticated \
    --memory 8Gi \
    --cpu 4 \
    --timeout 3600 \
    --set-env-vars PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1
```
*When the deployment finishes, copy the resulting Service URL (e.g., `https://wfc-worker-xyz.run.app`).*

### 3.3 Provision the Infrastructure (Terraform)
Navigate back to the `infrastructure/` directory in Cloud Shell.

1. Create a file named `terraform.tfvars`.
2. Add your specific variables:
   ```hcl
   project_id    = "your-new-gcp-project-id"
   cloud_run_url = "[https://wfc-worker-xyz.run.app](https://wfc-worker-xyz.run.app)"
   github_repo   = "YourGitHubUsername/wfc_cloud"
   ```
3. Initialize and apply the infrastructure:
   ```bash
   terraform init
   terraform apply
   ```

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false"}
## Critical Output Step
When Terraform finishes, look at the bottom of your terminal. It will output two green strings (`github_actions_service_account` and `workload_identity_provider`). Copy these! You need them for the CI/CD setup later.
:::

### 3.4 The Initial Frontend Deployment (Manual)
Before automating everything, let's deploy the frontend manually to establish a functional baseline. Terraform already initialized the App Engine architecture and granted it the necessary security permissions, so we just need to push the code.

Navigate to the `frontend/` directory in Cloud Shell:
```bash
cd ../frontend/
```

Deploy the frontend code:
```bash
gcloud app deploy
```

Once deployed, retrieve your live URL:
```bash
gcloud app browse
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 4: Testing the Baseline Architecture

It is time to see the decoupled architecture in action.

1. Open your App Engine frontend URL in a web browser.
2. Find a tiny pixel-art texture to use as a seed image. I would recommend using one of the [pixel art images here](https://github.com/mxgmn/WaveFunctionCollapse/tree/master/samples).
3. Upload the image, set the patch size to 3, and select an output size (e.g., 64x64).
4. Click **Add to Queue**.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## What is Happening Behind the Scenes?
* The frontend uploaded the image to Cloud Storage, wrote a "PENDING" document to Firestore, dropped a message into Pub/Sub, and immediately returned you to the dashboard.
* Your browser is now polling the `/jobs` route every 3 seconds, asking Firestore: *"Is it done yet?"*
* Meanwhile, Pub/Sub pushed the message to Cloud Run. Cloud Run is currently chewing through gigabytes of RAM to run the JIT-compiled Wave Function Collapse math.
* When Cloud Run finishes, it saves the image to the output bucket, updates Firestore to "COMPLETE", and your dashboard will instantly update to show the final generated image!
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 5: Exploring the Infrastructure UI

One of the best ways to understand cloud engineering is to step away from the code and view the raw data flowing through the infrastructure. Let's look under the hood.

### 5.1 The Firestore Database
1. In the Google Cloud Console search bar at the top, type **Firestore** and click on it.
2. Click on your `wfc-db` database. 
3. You should see a collection called `jobs`. Click on it.
4. Here, you will see the individual JSON-like documents created by your App Engine frontend. Notice the fields: you can see the user's chosen `patch_size`, the job `status` (which should now be `COMPLETE`), and the direct URLs to the resulting images!
5. Take a screenshot of the entries in your Firestore database. 

### 5.2 The Cloud Storage Buckets
1. Search for **Cloud Storage** and click on **Buckets**.
2. You will see the two buckets Terraform created: `wfc-inputs-[PROJECT_ID]` and `wfc-outputs-[PROJECT_ID]`. 
3. Click into the outputs bucket. You can see the actual `.png` files your Cloud Run worker generated and saved. Because Terraform attached a "publicly readable" IAM policy to this bucket, your frontend dashboard is allowed to display these images directly to users.

### 5.3 The Cloud Run Worker Logs
1. Search for **Cloud Run** and click on your `wfc-worker` service.
2. Click on the **Logs** tab near the top.
3. This is where your backend's Python `print()` statements go! You can see the exact millisecond the Pub/Sub queue woke up the worker, how long the heavy math took to execute, and the moment it successfully uploaded the final result.
4. Take a screenshot of the cloud run worker logs. 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 6: Automation & CI/CD

Now that you know the architecture works, let's wire up the CI/CD pipeline so you never have to manually deploy code again. 

### 6.1 Configure GitHub Actions
1. Open `.github/workflows/deploy-frontend.yml` and `.github/workflows/deploy-worker.yml`.
2. Replace the placeholder strings for `workload_identity_provider` and `service_account` with the green outputs you copied from Terraform earlier.

### 6.2 Triggering the Auto-Deploy (The Magic)
Let's prove the pipeline works by making a visible change to the frontend.

1. Open `frontend/templates/index.html` in the Cloud Shell Editor.
2. Find the `<h1>Wave Function Collapse</h1>` tag near the top and make a change to it. 
3. Commit and push these changes to GitHub using Cloud Shell:
   ```bash
   git add .
   git commit -m "Configure CI/CD and update title"
   git push origin main
   ```
4. Go to the **Actions** tab in your GitHub repository online. You should see the App Engine deployment running automatically! 
5. When the action turns green, refresh your live App Engine URL. You should instantly see your new custom title. 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-important collapse="false"}
## Lab Submission Requirements

To complete this capstone lab, please submit:

1. A link to your forked GitHub repository showing the passing GitHub Actions workflows.
2. The URL to your live App Engine frontend.
3. A screenshot of your dashboard showing your custom `<h1>` title and at least two successfully completed WFC generations of different output sizes.
4. A screenshot of your Cloud Run worker logs. 
5. A screenshot of a few entries in the Firestore database. 
6. A few sentences explaining *why* we used Pub/Sub to decouple the frontend from the backend, instead of just having App Engine run the math directly.
7. Briefly describe the main componenents of the wave function collapse GitHub repo. You won't need to understand any of the specifics of the code, but in broad terms, what do  directories like `infrastructure`, `frontend`, `worker`, and `.github/workflows`? Broadly speaking, what are the functions of the files in these directories?
:::
